focusLanes.title: Accessibility Robotics
focusLanes.copy: OBSERV-E, GRVI, DRVI, and HRVI connect assistive robotics with guidance designed around dignity, patience, and direct user feedback.
points: Guide robot for visually impaired users
Drone support for broader perception
Haptic receiver with direct Bluetooth cues

focusLanes.title: Applied AI
focusLanes.copy: Models, agents, and voice systems shaped into tools people can understand, trust, and use.
points: LLM and speech prototypes
Classification and anomaly detection
Workflows that connect to useful outcomes

focusLanes.title: Robotics
focusLanes.copy: Real-time validation, perception, and autonomy work grounded in safer movement.
points: Sensor fusion with RTMaps + C++
Lead vehicle and driver monitoring work
Requirements, testing, and integration coverage

focusLanes.title: Cybersecurity
focusLanes.copy: Operational tools that reduce manual effort, improve visibility, and give teams more room for judgment.
points: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC automation
Reporting and downstream integrations
Monitoring-minded architecture

focusLanes.title: Product Systems
focusLanes.copy: Cloud tooling, product clarity, and practical delivery for ideas that need to become dependable.

Internal tools and AI-facing product work
Interfaces designed around clarity and useful routing

snapshots.label: Budget approval rate as robotics treasurer
Portfolio avatar direction with browser speech

projects.title: OBSERV-E Accessibility Robotics Ecosystem
projects.summary: OBSERV-E is an accessibility robotics umbrella focused on helping visually impaired users perceive, navigate, and receive environmental guidance through robots, drones, and haptic feedback.
projects.showcase: A chapter about turning robotics curiosity into service: GRVI, DRVI, and HRVI as one connected accessibility system.
projects.visualHint: Robot vision, direct Bluetooth signaling, and haptic user feedback.
outcomes: Reframed the architecture from robot → web app → end user to direct robot → end user Bluetooth signaling for faster feedback.
Defined OBSERV-E as the umbrella ecosystem that includes GRVI, DRVI, and HRVI instead of treating each robot as an isolated project.
Built the pitch direction around service-dog-inspired guidance, computer vision, and user-centered support for visually impaired people.

proof.robotics: OBSERV-E connects mobile robotics, aerial robotics, and haptic receivers into one assistive navigation ecosystem.
proof.cpp: Future embedded control and signal handling can connect robot state to haptic output.
proof.python: Prototype logic and perception experiments can be supported through Python-based robotics tooling.

detailCards.title: GRVI · Guide Robot for the Visually Impaired
detailCards.note: Ground robot that acts like a robotic guide companion, using perception to detect obstacles and route guidance cues to the user.

detailCards.title: DRVI · Drone Robot for the Visually Impaired
detailCards.note: Aerial support robot concept for broader environmental awareness, scouting, and overhead perception when ground-level sensing is limited.

detailCards.title: HRVI · Haptic Receiver for the Visually Impaired
detailCards.note: Wearable or handheld receiver that turns robot signals into direct tactile feedback for the end user through Bluetooth.

projects.title: Selvam Valuations · ScarletHacks M&A Intelligence Prototype
projects.summary: Built a team M&A intelligence prototype at ScarletHacks, exploring financial-data workflows, LLM-assisted article analysis, valuation context, ESG-style graphing, and merger-lab comparisons.
projects.showcase: A financial AI prototype shaped around domain research, careful assumptions, product judgment, API planning, and deployment/debugging awareness.
projects.visualHint: Financial data, news context, LLM insight extraction, valuation assumptions, and merger comparison.
outcomes: Created a team prototype for M&A/valuation intelligence during ScarletHacks.
Explored how LLM-assisted summaries and structured data can support company comparison and deal reasoning.
Identified practical reliability issues around live APIs, CORS, environment variables, and deployment.

proof.python: Project work involved practical prototype logic and demo-ready implementation.
proof.apis: Hackathon builds often required connecting product interface, backend, and service workflows quickly.
proof.product: The project emphasized product value and explainability, not only technical implementation.

detailCards.title: Financial AI product concept
detailCards.note: Company data, article analysis, valuation context, and merger-lab workflows.

detailCards.title: Backend and API planning

detailCards.title: Deployment lessons
detailCards.note: CORS, API keys, Vercel/FastAPI structure, and the difference between prototype and production reliability.

projects.title: Monocular Lane Detection Improvement Study
projects.summary: Compared backbone architectures and data augmentation strategies for lane detection, simplifying the original 3D problem into a feasible 2D lane-coordinate prediction pipeline.
projects.showcase: Research-style ML project comparing robustness, accuracy, and efficiency tradeoffs with humility about scope and evidence.
projects.visualHint: Backbone comparison, augmentation impact, and SALAD-style evaluation.
outcomes: Compared ResNet-18, EfficientNet-B0, and MobileNetV2 to study accuracy versus efficiency.
Used augmentation such as blur, noise, brightness changes, and shadow simulation to test robustness.
Presented F1-score, precision, recall, and center-distance error to explain strengths and weaknesses clearly.

proof.python: Training, preprocessing, metrics, and plotting were handled in Python.
proof.sklearn: Evaluation used structured metrics and comparison tables to explain model behavior.
proof.ml: The project focused on model performance, robustness, and architecture tradeoffs.

detailCards.title: Backbone comparison
detailCards.note: ResNet-18 baseline versus EfficientNet-B0 and MobileNetV2.

detailCards.title: Augmentation study
detailCards.note: Weather and lighting-inspired transforms tested model robustness.

detailCards.title: Results narrative
detailCards.note: Explain F1-score, precision, recall, and CD error in terms of lane detection quality.

projects.title: A Little Tech For You · In-progress AI Guide for Older Adults
projects.summary: A friendly AI education and tech-support platform that helps older adults understand everyday technology through simple chats, short videos, accessible explainers, and private consulting workflows.
projects.showcase: Accessible AI product in progress for older adults and families who need patient, practical, plain-language technology guidance.
projects.visualHint: Question → simple explanation → short tutorial → optional consulting support.
outcomes: Defined the product around a real audience: older adults who want practical help with phones, AI, Bluetooth, smart TVs, car technology, online safety, and everyday tech questions.
Designed the app as a mix of short tutorial videos, simple chat answers, consulting paths, and membership options rather than a generic chatbot.
Planned an AI voice narration pipeline using a Python/FastAPI voice service with Hugging Face TTS models so tutorial content can sound patient, clear, and personal.

proof.ai-tools: Uses LLMs and AI-assisted development to rapidly prototype user flows, scripts, explanations, and knowledge-base content.
proof.huggingface: Planned voice-service pipeline uses ResembleAI/chatterbox as a primary TTS model and coqui/XTTS-v2 as a fallback for cloned-voice narration.
proof.accessibility: Product framing centers clarity, patience, plain language, large readable UI, and support paths for non-technical users.

detailCards.title: Simple tech guidance
detailCards.note: Older adults can ask technology questions and get patient, plain-language answers that avoid jargon.

detailCards.title: Short tutorial content
detailCards.note: Tutorial scripts can become short videos with AI-generated narration and step-by-step visuals.

detailCards.title: Consulting and membership path
detailCards.note: The project includes room for private consulting, memberships, and family support options.

projects.title: Reinforcement Learning Lane-Keeping Simulator
projects.summary: Built a lightweight lane-keeping simulator to compare sensor configurations and reward design for autonomous-driving-style reinforcement learning behavior.
projects.showcase: Explores rewards, crashes, lane deviation, completion rate, and simulated sensor tradeoffs.
projects.visualHint: Policy, reward, sensor stack, and lane-keeping behavior.
outcomes: Compared Camera, Camera+LiDAR, and full-stack sensing configurations using driving metrics.
Tracked reward, collision rate, off-road rate, task completion, lane deviation, and distance traveled.
Used simulator videos and metric tables to explain why more sensor data is not always better for RL.

proof.python: The custom simulator, training loop, and evaluation scripts were built in Python.
proof.ml: PPO training connected policy learning to reward feedback and state observations.
proof.robotics: The task modeled autonomous lane keeping and sensor-based decision making.

detailCards.title: Reward design
detailCards.note: Show how good lane position, crashes, and off-road behavior affected learning.

detailCards.title: Sensor comparison
detailCards.note: Camera versus Camera+LiDAR versus full stack.

detailCards.title: Demo video slot
detailCards.note: Use for the cleanest driving replay or the most informative failure case.

projects.title: TTP DNS Screening & Outreach Automation
projects.summary: A Streamlit-based business workflow that scans domains for SPF, DKIM, and DMARC issues, generates tailored outreach, and supports safe test/live email sending through Microsoft Graph.
projects.showcase: Built to make a technical DNS scanner usable by a non-technical business team through a simple web interface.
projects.visualHint: CSV contacts, DNS scanner, SQLite logging, Graph API sending, and Streamlit controls.
outcomes: Designed a workflow that can test-send or live-send while keeping the Streamlit app stable for larger batches.
Connected DNS security findings to plain-language outreach messaging for business use.
Separated test overrides from prospect outreach to reduce mistakes during demos and production runs.

proof.security: Finds SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configuration issues that impact email trust.
proof.automation: Combines scanning, contact ingestion, templating, and Microsoft Graph sending.
proof.product: Wraps technical automation in a Streamlit interface designed for a boss or client to use.

detailCards.note: Runs domain checks and turns technical DNS results into structured findings.

points: Building a DNS/security outreach automation pipeline around SPF, DKIM, DMARC, dark-web indicators, Microsoft Graph email delivery, and tracking workflows.
Developing Python tooling that turns manual client security checks into repeatable reporting and outreach operations.
Designing structured CSV/JSON outputs, database persistence, and validation steps against external tools such as EasyDMARC.
Developed AI instructor and training-support prototypes using Azure Cognitive Services, Speech Services, Azure Functions, and web tooling.
Automated and supported a 50+ computer training fleet with Python, PowerShell, and operational scripting.
Contributed to AIHeadShotMasters production workflows involving Production App Workflows, Supabase, Vercel CI/CD, Stripe checkout, and debugging deployment issues.

experience.role: Connected Automated Vehicle Engineer · Sensor Fusion Lead
points: Authored requirements and validation test cases for perception, lead-vehicle detection, and driver-monitoring modules.
Implemented real-time C++ and RTMaps workflows across camera, radar, LiDAR, and vehicle-state signals.
Built practical understanding of autonomy systems, signal routing, and test-driven validation for connected automated vehicles.
Defining OBSERV-E as an accessibility robotics umbrella for GRVI, DRVI, and HRVI.
Shifting the architecture toward direct Bluetooth robot-to-user feedback rather than relying on a web app as the main signal path.
Building pitch materials around service-dog-inspired robotics, computer vision, haptics, and accessible navigation.
Secured budget approvals and coordinated purchasing, workshops, and student robotics operations.
Led and supported workshops across AI/ML, Linux, Python, systems programming, and robotics fundamentals.
Represented Illinois Tech Robotics in competitions and collaborative projects, including a winning StarkHacks 2026 build.

Serves in student leadership contexts that connect engineering, communication, and community impact.
Uses leadership training to strengthen project planning, technical storytelling, and team accountability.

avatarReplies.intro: I’m Danish Nadar. I build AI systems, automation workflows, robotics software, and security-focused tooling that aim to create real value outside of a demo environment.
avatarReplies.build: My work sits at the intersection of applied AI, robotics, and practical product engineering. That includes voice prototypes, ML systems, sensor fusion, and automation tools for real workflows.
avatarReplies.hire: You hire me when you want someone who can think across model logic, system integration, validation, and user-facing delivery instead of treating those as separate worlds.
avatarReplies.next: Right now I’m focused on AI engineering roles where I can help move systems from prototype to production and keep building stronger depth in useful, real-world applications.
